channel.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing
channel.description: Free daily agricultural market briefing for corn, soybean, and grain producers. Overnight surprises, farmer actions, market analysis — every morning at 5 AM CT.

image.title: AGSIST Daily — Morning Agricultural Intelligence Briefing

item.title: GRAINS MIXED AS PLANTING PRESSURE BUILDS
item.description: A week of choppy trading left corn at $4.41/bu as of Friday's close, down 7 cents, while soybeans found their footing at $11.76/bu, up 8 cents. The divergence reflects growing anxiety about planting windows — corn needs dirt time now, while soybeans can wait. Corn slipped while soybeans gained ground as farmers eye critical weather windows ahead. • WEEK IN REVIEW → Use any corn rallies next week to price remaining bushels — market confidence can evaporate quickly with weather. • LIVESTOCK DIVERGENCE → Cattle producers must protect these historic margins immediately — this level of strength rarely lasts. • WHAT TO WATCH MONDAY → Have contingency pricing plans ready — weather-driven moves can be swift and significant during planting season. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-11

item.title: GRAINS STEADY AS PLANTING WINDOW OPENS
item.description: Corn held $4.43 while soybeans barely budged at $11.68 as traders await the real action: planting season weather patterns. With optimal corn planting starting Monday across the Corn Belt, every forecast becomes market-moving. Chicago wheat fell a penny to $5.71 on global competition concerns. Markets mark time ahead of crucial weather-driven weeks. • Grains & Oilseeds → Monitor 7-14 day forecasts religiously — pricing windows will open and close rapidly based on weather. • Livestock & Dairy → Cattle producers should price remaining unhedged inventory — these historically strong levels deserve protection. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs immediately — current levels are manageable before seasonal demand spike. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-10

item.title: STOCKS SURGE AS GRAINS INCH HIGHER
item.description: Corn futures gained 1.25¢ to $4.50/bu while soybeans added 4.25¢ to $11.66/bu in quiet trade, as attention shifted to the S&P 500's 2.5% rally. The modest grain gains reflect steady underlying demand as planting season approaches, with weather remaining the key wild card. Broad-based gains suggest improving market confidence heading into peak planting season. • Grains & Oilseeds → Hold current positions and monitor 7-day weather forecasts — any planting delays could quickly shift price dynamics. • Livestock & Dairy → Cattle producers should consider locking some protection on these historically strong margins. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs now — current levels are workable but vulnerable to spring demand spikes. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-09

item.title: CRUDE OIL DROPS 3% AS GRAINS DRIFT
item.description: Crude oil's 3.3% overnight drop to $93.27 signals fresh volatility in input costs just as corn planting season hits full stride. Corn barely budged at $4.44/bu, down just half a cent, while soybeans managed a modest 2.5-cent gain to $11.57/bu. The divergence between energy weakness and grain stability suggests markets are calibrating for different spring narratives. Energy volatility returns while corn holds near $4.44 ahead of planting push. • Grains & Oilseeds → Hold current positions and prep for planting — this stability suggests good underlying support. • Livestock & Dairy → Consider locking protection on current positions — volatility suggests more chop ahead. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs immediately — this dip won't last once planting demand hits. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-08

item.title: LEAN HOGS SURGE 3% ON TIGHT SUPPLIES
item.description: Lean hogs jumped 3.1% to $107.70 in the overnight session, the strongest move across agricultural markets as tight hog supplies collide with steady pork demand. Nearby corn eased 1.75 cents to $4.52 per bushel while soybeans held nearly flat at $11.68, with traders shifting attention from price action to weather forecasts as planting season approaches. Grains drift sideways as focus shifts to planting conditions ahead. • Grains & Oilseeds → Hold current positions and monitor 7-day forecasts closely — planting delays could quickly shift this market dynamic. • Livestock & Dairy → Hog producers should price unpriced inventory immediately — this volatility suggests more upside potential with limited downside protection. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs now — current levels are manageable but vulnerable to demand-driven spikes during peak planting. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-07

item.title: GRAINS GAIN GROUND AS PLANTING WINDOW OPENS
item.description: Corn gained 2.75¢ to $4.51/bu this morning while soybeans added 8.5¢ to $11.68/bu as the grain complex found its footing after last week's profit-taking. With optimal planting window opening in two weeks, weather premiums are starting to creep back into the market even on these modest moves. Corn, beans, and wheat all move higher on modest buying interest ahead of critical planting season. • Grains & Oilseeds → Hold current positions but watch planting weather closely — this bounce suggests good underlying demand support. • Livestock & Dairy → Cattle producers should consider locking some protection on these strong margins — volatility tends to increase from here. • Energy & Inputs → Lock remaining diesel needs if you haven't already — current levels are workable but vulnerable to spring demand spikes. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-06

item.title: ENERGY RALLY PAUSES — GRAINS READY FOR MONDAY TEST
item.description: Friday's grain weakness — corn dropped to $4.52/bu and beans fell to $11.63/bu — sets up a critical test when markets reopen Monday morning. With crude oil holding near $112 despite energy's pause, farmers face elevated input costs just as planting pressure builds across the Upper Midwest. Crude's 7% surge cools but leaves input costs elevated as planting season intensifies. • WEEK AHEAD: GRAIN REBOUND WATCH → Hold current positions but watch Monday's open closely — buying interest below $4.50 corn would signal strong technical support. • LIVESTOCK DIVERGENCE CONTINUES → Cattle producers should consider pricing protection on current positions — margins are historically strong but volatility is increasing. • MONDAY WATCH LIST: INPUT COSTS → Lock remaining diesel needs now — current levels are workable but vulnerable to geopolitical spikes during driving season. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-05

item.title: CRUDE EXPLODES 7% — GRAINS SLIP ON PROFIT-TAKING
item.description: Crude oil exploded 7% higher to $112.06 overnight, the biggest energy move in months, while grains retreated modestly with corn down 5¢ to $4.52 and soybeans off 9¼¢ to $11.63. The energy spike threatens to add $15-20 per acre to diesel and fertilizer costs just as corn planting begins across the upper Midwest. Meanwhile, grains are taking a breather after strong March gains, with December corn still holding above $4.81 despite today's pullback. Energy surge adds $15/acre to input costs while corn and beans ease after recent rally. • Grains Pause Rally → Hold current positions — this looks like profit-taking, not trend change. Watch Monday for buying interest on the dip. • Livestock Mixed Signals → Cattle feeders should price protection on current positions — feed cost inflation accelerating. • Energy Shock → Lock diesel prices now if you haven't already — this energy spike has legs and spring demand is about to peak. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-03

item.title: HOGS EXPLODE 22% AS WHEAT DROPS 22¢
item.description: Lean hogs exploded 22.1% to $105.20 in overnight trading — the kind of move that happens maybe twice a decade — while Chicago wheat dropped 22¢ to $5.97/bu on improving global supply prospects. Corn eased 5½¢ to $4.53/bu and soybeans slipped 6¢ to $11.67/bu as the market shifts focus from South American harvest delays to Midwest planting conditions. With optimal corn planting just two weeks away, weather will soon trump everything else. Disease fears trigger historic hog rally while grain complex softens ahead of planting season. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold current pricing positions. Weather premium building season starts in two weeks — don't get caught short if conditions turn. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Hog producers: Lock profits immediately on this rally. Cattle feeders: Monitor feed costs but margins remain healthy. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock diesel prices now if you haven't already — this relief may be temporary with summer driving season ahead. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-02

item.title: CATTLE SURGE 4.2% IGNITES BROAD RALLY
item.description: Live cattle exploded 4.2% higher to $243.30, leading a broad commodity rally that pulled grains and oils along for the ride. Soybeans gained 12½¢ to $11.72 while corn added 2½¢ to $4.58, but the real story is growing protein demand meeting tight cattle supplies. With April planting season opening, input costs remain manageable despite energy's mixed signals. Grains climb with live cattle while precious metals spike on broader risk appetite. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold current positions — this looks like technical buying, not demand-driven strength. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Consider forward-selling cattle on this rally — margins are attractive and volatility is high. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in fuel now if you haven't — crude at $102 is workable but vulnerable to geopolitical spikes. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-04-01

item.title: CORN SLIDES 8¢ AS CRUDE JUMPS 3%
item.description: Corn dropped 8¢ to $4.55/bu overnight while WTI crude spiked 3.2% to $104.84, creating a classic squeeze on farm margins just as spring fieldwork ramps up. The divergence highlights the seasonal tug-of-war between energy costs and grain prices that defines March profitability. December corn at $4.83/bu held better than nearby, suggesting the market still expects seasonal strength through summer. Energy surge squeezes grain margins while cattle rally ahead of planting season. • Grains Under Pressure → Hold off on new-crop corn sales until this technical selling exhausts itself — likely within a week. • Cattle Rally Strong → Consider hedging cattle positions — this rally may have limited upside with feed costs climbing. • Energy Costs Surge → Lock diesel contracts now if you haven't — this crude rally has more room to run. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-31

item.title: CRUDE EXPLODES ABOVE $101, GRAINS SLIDE
item.description: Crude oil surged to $101.18 per barrel overnight — an 8.3% jump that's putting immediate pressure on input costs while corn slid 5¼¢ to $4.62 and soybeans dropped 15¼¢ to $11.60. The energy spike comes at the worst possible time for spring field prep, with nitrogen applications starting and diesel demand about to ramp. USDA's Prospective Plantings report looms at month-end. Energy costs spike 8.3% while corn and beans ease on profit-taking ahead of planting report. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold pricing decisions until Thursday's plantings data — this dip could reverse quickly if intended acres disappoint. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Consider forward contracting cattle if you're current — this rally has legs but higher feed costs will eventually matter. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in remaining diesel needs immediately — this crude rally could have more room to run with spring demand hitting. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-30

item.title: QUIET FRIDAY KEEPS GRAINS STEADY
item.description: Grain markets closed another sleepy Friday with corn at $4.66 per bushel, down just 1¼¢, and soybeans easing 4¢ to $11.71. The lack of drama is typical pre-planting season — traders waiting for acreage intentions data while farmers gear up for fieldwork. Energy costs climbed with crude oil gaining 1.8%, adding pressure to input expenses. Oats jump 2% on small volume while corn and beans drift lower ahead of weekend. • Grains Stay Calm → Use the calm to evaluate new-crop sales — December corn above $4.90 covers most production costs. • Livestock Edges Higher → Monitor feed cost ratios — current levels support maintaining herd sizes through spring. • Energy Costs Climb → Consider locking diesel prices if you haven't — crude oil momentum looks bullish short-term. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-27

item.title: GRAINS RALLY AHEAD OF PLANTING REPORT
item.description: Grain markets found their footing Thursday with corn up 7½¢ to $4.67 and soybeans gaining nearly 20¢ to $11.73. The rally comes as crude oil spiked 2.5% to $91.13, adding input cost pressure while traders position ahead of the USDA's critical Prospective Plantings report due next week. With fieldwork starting in southern areas and nitrogen applications beginning across the Corn Belt, the market is pricing in both input inflation and potential acreage surprises. Corn, soybeans, and wheat all gained as energy costs climbed and pre-plant positioning heats up. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Consider pricing 10-15% of new-crop corn on this rally if you haven't already — pre-plant strength often fades once actual acres are confirmed. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Cattle feeders should monitor corn basis closely — local elevator premiums likely to widen as planting approaches. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in any remaining diesel and fertilizer needs immediately — energy momentum suggests higher input costs ahead. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-26

item.title: METALS EXPLODE WHILE GRAINS DRIFT SIDEWAYS
item.description: Markets split dramatically overnight — precious metals exploded higher while grains marked time. Silver spiked to $71.75, up 7.1%, dragging gold along for a 3.2% rally to $4,487. Meanwhile, corn managed just a quarter-cent gain to $4.62/bu as traders wait for USDA's planting intentions report. The divergence highlights how grain markets are stuck in neutral while global uncertainty drives flight-to-safety trades. Silver surged 7.1% overnight as corn barely budged ahead of planting intentions. • Grains Mark Time → Hold pricing decisions until Friday's report — no urgency to move on these modest changes. • Livestock Softens → Dairy producers should consider forward contracting 15-20% of spring production on this milk rally. • Energy Retreats → Lock diesel prices for spring operations if offered fixed contracts — this dip may not last. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-25

item.title: CRUDE OIL CRATERS 10% — GRAIN COMPLEX FOLLOWS
item.description: Crude oil's stunning 10.3% overnight collapse triggered a sell-off across grains, with corn down 9½¢ and wheat off 13¾¢. The energy crash signals potential relief for diesel and fertilizer costs, but also raises recession fears that could crush export demand. With Prospective Plantings looming, this volatility hits at the worst possible time for spring planning decisions. Energy collapse drags corn and wheat lower as input cost relief battles demand destruction fears. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold off on major pricing decisions until energy markets stabilize — volatility this extreme rarely lasts. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Cattle feeders should consider locking in feed costs if this grain weakness holds through the week. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Don't rush into fertilizer purchases — let dealers adjust prices first, but start having conversations about locking lower rates. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-24

item.title: GOLD CRASHES HARDEST IN YEARS
item.description: Gold plunged 4.9% overnight — its worst drop since 2022 — while silver got obliterated with an 8.6% collapse as institutional money fled safe havens. The metals massacre coincided with crude oil spiking 4.6% to $98 on escalating Middle East tensions, creating a perfect storm for input costs. Meanwhile, live cattle jumped 2.5% as protein demand stays strong despite grain pressure. Precious metals bloodbath sends cattle soaring as crude oil spikes on Middle East tensions. • GRAINS UNDER PRESSURE → Hold current grain sales — this looks like financial selling, not fundamental weakness. • LIVESTOCK DIVERGES SHARPLY → Consider forward contracting cattle if you have them — this move has legs. • ENERGY SPIKE HITS INPUTS → Lock in diesel prices this week if you haven't already — $98 crude won't stay here long either direction. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-23

item.title: MEAL ROCKETS 2.3% ON CHINESE CRUSH DEMAND
item.description: Soybean meal exploded 2.3% overnight as Chinese crushers scrambled for protein ahead of their seasonal rebuild. The meal rally dragged soybeans up 8½¢ despite lackluster export inspections. Meanwhile, gold and silver got hammered — down 4.1% and 4.4% respectively — as the dollar flexed and speculators fled commodities. Soybeans follow meal higher while precious metals crater on dollar strength. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Consider pricing 10-15% of new-crop beans on this meal-driven rally — protein premiums won't last forever. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Lock in milk pricing if you can — feed costs are climbing faster than meat prices. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Watch for diesel price drops in 1-2 weeks — good timing for spring fieldwork prep. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-20

item.title: ENERGY SHOCK LIFTS GRAINS AS NATGAS ROCKETS 8.4%
item.description: Energy markets exploded overnight with natural gas up 8.4% and crude jumping $5.50 to $97.75 — the biggest energy shock in months. Corn responded with a 11¢ rally to $4.63, wheat jumped 19¼¢, and soybean meal surged 2.8% as markets price in higher fertilizer and diesel costs. With planting season weeks away, this energy surge couldn't come at a worse time for input planning. Crude up $5+ overnight sends input costs soaring while corn rallies on production concerns. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Lock diesel fuel for spring work immediately — prices climbing 15-20¢/gallon this week. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Cattle feeders should consider locking Q2 margins — energy rally supports beef values. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Secure remaining nitrogen needs immediately — prices climbing $50-75/ton this week. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-19

item.title: HOGS EXPLODE 25% ON SUPPLY SHOCK
item.description: Lean hogs rocketed 25% overnight — the biggest single-day move in two years — as African Swine Fever reports from key production regions sent shockwaves through protein markets. Meanwhile, wheat collapsed 15¼¢ as Ukrainian exports flooded the market just as Upper Midwest producers finalize spring planting plans. The dollar's retreat is helping corn and beans hold support above key technical levels. Wheat tumbles 15¢ as Black Sea competition intensifies before spring planting decisions. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold current corn positions — this defensive action suggests good underlying demand. Consider spring wheat acreage cuts if margins look tight. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Watch for ASF confirmation reports — could dramatically alter corn demand projections for 2026-27. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in diesel and propane needs for spring — current levels may be as good as it gets this season. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-18

item.title: LIVESTOCK EXPLODES AS GRAIN COMPLEX CRATERS
item.description: The agricultural complex split violently overnight with livestock futures exploding higher while grains and oilseeds cratered. Lean hogs rocketed 8.6% to $93.50 — the biggest single-session move in over a year — while soybeans collapsed 3.8% to $11.55 as fund liquidation accelerated. The divergence creates a massive shift in feed cost dynamics just as spring planting decisions lock in. Hogs surge 8.6% overnight while soybeans collapse 3.8% in stunning reversal that flips feed cost math. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold pricing decisions — this looks like technical washout, not fundamental shift. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Consider forward pricing summer hogs if you're unhedged — this momentum could continue. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in diesel prices now if you haven't — this energy weakness might not last. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-17

item.title: CRUDE SPIKES 4%, MILK EXPLODES 7% OVERNIGHT
item.description: Crude oil's unexpected 4% overnight rally to $99.31 signals rising input costs just as spring field prep begins, while Class III milk's shocking 7% jump to $17.30 reshuffles livestock feed economics. Corn managed a modest 9¢ gain to $4.67, but the real story is how energy and dairy volatility will ripple through your spring planting decisions. Energy rally pressures input costs while dairy surge creates livestock feed dynamic shift. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Consider locking diesel prices now before crude breaks $100 — grain prices haven't caught up to input cost reality yet. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → If you're considering livestock expansion, dairy operations now offer significantly better margins than beef. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Prioritize diesel and liquid fertilizer purchases this week before crude breaks $100 — prices only go one way from here. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-16

item.title: OATS RALLY 4½¢ WHILE STOCKS CRATER
item.description: Oats jumped 4½¢ to $3.71 while the S&P 500 cratered 1.5% in its worst session since December. Corn held surprisingly steady despite new-crop December slipping 2¾¢ as pre-plant positioning intensifies. With fieldwork starting across the South and Prospective Plantings three weeks out, the real question isn't today's moves—it's whether this financial market chaos changes planting intentions. Wall Street's worst day in months sends safe-haven flows into commodities as spring planting window opens. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold pricing decisions until Prospective Plantings—too much volatility to chase today's moves. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Dairy producers should lock in feed costs on any grain weakness—margins are too tight for speculation. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in diesel needs now if you haven't—oil's momentum suggests higher prices ahead. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-13

item.title: CRUDE OIL ROCKETS 11% HIGHER OVERNIGHT
item.description: Crude oil exploded 11.2% higher overnight to $92.16, dragging natural gas (+7.2%) and diesel prices sharply higher just as spring fieldwork approaches. The energy rally lifted corn 8¼¢ to $4.61 and sent oats screaming 15¢ higher (+4.3%) on ethanol optimism and higher input costs. Cattle dropped 1-1.7% as higher feed and energy costs squeezed margins. Energy surge lifts corn and oats while cattle slide on higher feed costs. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Watch diesel prices today — if they follow crude higher, your spring input costs just jumped. Price some corn if margins still work. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Cattle feeders should consider hedging more summer cattle — this margin squeeze isn't going away quickly. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock in remaining fuel and nitrogen needs TODAY if you haven't already — this energy rally has legs. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-12

item.title: HOGS SPIKE 11% ON PORK EXPORT SURGE
item.description: Lean hogs exploded 11.2% overnight — the biggest single-day move in eight months — as surprise Asian demand hit just as seasonal production tightens. Corn rode the coattails with feed demand optimism, gaining 4¢ to $4.54. But dairy told a different story: Class III milk plunged 5.1% as spring flush production meets weakening demand. Corn and beans follow with modest gains while milk crashes on oversupply concerns. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Watch April hog futures — if they hold above $96, consider pricing 10-15% of stored corn on this feed demand story. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Hog producers should price aggressively on this rally — moves like this don't last long. • ENERGY & INPUTS → No immediate action — diesel savings help but won't change planting decisions already made. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-11

item.title: CRUDE OIL CRASHES 24% — GRAINS FOLLOW DEEPER
item.description: Crude oil's 24% overnight crash triggered a broad commodity selloff, with corn losing 21¢ to $4.53 and wheat plunging 35¢ to $6.03. The energy collapse is pulling down input costs but also crushing the entire commodity risk appetite. This isn't just an oil story — it's a liquidity event hitting everything from grain to cattle. Corn drops 21¢ and wheat collapses 35¢ as energy rout ripples through commodity complex. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Don't panic-sell stored grain on this energy-driven selloff — fundamentals remain supportive. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Calculate new feed cost ratios — today's grain drop may have improved your livestock margins. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Reassess fertilizer purchasing — energy crash may have created buying opportunities for spring application. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-10

item.title: CRUDE EXPLODES 14%, WHEAT SURGES 5.6%
item.description: Crude oil's 14% overnight explosion is reshaping your input costs — fertilizer and diesel are about to get expensive fast. Meanwhile, wheat's 33¢ surge signals serious supply concerns, pulling corn and beans along for the ride. This isn't just a commodity rally; it's an inflation warning shot three weeks before planting season. Energy shock ripples through ag inputs as wheat catches fire on Black Sea tensions. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Consider pricing 10-15% of stored corn on this rally — momentum could carry through USDA's March 31 planting report. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Cattle feeders should lock feed costs immediately — corn rally plus energy shock creates perfect storm for margins. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock remaining nitrogen needs immediately — this energy surge will flow through to fertilizer within days. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-09

item.title: CORN RALLIES 9¢ ON PLANTING UNCERTAINTY
item.description: Corn futures jumped 9¢ overnight to $4.54 as traders price in planting delays from soggy Midwest fields. Chicago wheat surged 14¢ to $5.85 on export demand, while crude oil's 2.6% rally to $79.34 signals fertilizer and diesel costs aren't done climbing. The overnight moves suggest nervous money is building positions ahead of USDA's March 31 planting intentions report. Wheat jumps 14¢ while crude oil surge signals input cost pressure ahead of spring fieldwork. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Consider pricing 15-20% of new-crop corn on this rally above $4.75 Dec futures. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Dairy producers should lock Q2 milk prices above $17 if possible. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Secure diesel and NH3 needs for next 60 days before prices climb further. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-06

item.title: CATTLE SURGE OVERNIGHT, GRAINS DRIFT LOWER
item.description: Livestock markets caught fire overnight with cattle futures up nearly 2% on renewed beef demand strength, while grains drifted lower in lackluster trade. The dollar's weakness couldn't spark export interest in corn or beans, suggesting domestic fundamentals are driving this market. With March planting intentions looming, producers face critical acreage decisions in a sideways price environment. Live cattle jumped 1.8% on stronger beef demand signals while corn and wheat lost ground ahead of spring planting decisions. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold current pricing positions — no compelling reason to act on today's modest moves. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Livestock producers should consider protecting these higher prices — cattle rallies this sharp often correct quickly. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Delay additional nitrogen purchases if possible — prices should improve through March. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-05

item.title: CRUDE RALLIES 3% AS GRAINS DRIFT SIDEWAYS
item.description: Crude oil jumped $2.10 to $74.80 on geopolitical tensions, pushing diesel and fertilizer costs higher just as spring fieldwork approaches. Meanwhile, corn and soybeans barely budged despite the energy rally, suggesting grain markets are waiting for clearer planting signals. Gold and silver got hammered — down 5.2% and 8.4% respectively — as the dollar flexed muscle. Energy surge boosts input costs while precious metals crater on dollar strength ahead of planting season. • GRAINS & OILSEEDS → Hold current positions — market lacks conviction until USDA Prospective Plantings. • LIVESTOCK & DAIRY → Milk producers should hedge Q2 production — this drop looks overdone. • ENERGY & INPUTS → Lock remaining diesel needs now — crude momentum looks sticky. Read more: https://agsist.com/daily/2026-03-04
